perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "Affiliated Company" of a Party shall mean any other legal entity: · directly or indirectly owning or controlling the Party (Mother company), or · under the direct or indirect ownership or control of the same legal entity (Mother company) directly or indirectly owning or controlling the Party, or · directly or indirectly owned or controlled by the Party, for as long as such ownership or control lasts. "Ownership" or "control" shall exist through the direct or indirect ownership of more than fifty (50) percent of the nominal value of the issued equity share capital or of more than fifty (50) percent of the shares entitling the holders to vote for the election of directors or persons performing similar functions.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "Affiliated Company" of a Party shall mean any other legal entity: · directly or indirectly owning or controlling the Party (Mother company), or · under the direct or indirect ownership or control of the same legal entity (Mother company) directly or indirectly owning or controlling the Party, or · directly or indirectly owned or controlled by the Party, for as long as such ownership or control lasts. "Ownership" or "control" shall exist through the direct or indirect ownership of more than twenty-five (25) percent of the nominal value of the issued equity share capital or of more than twenty-five (25) percent of the shares entitling the holders to vote for the election of directors or persons performing similar functions.
perturbation.explanation: The perturbation introduces an in-text contradiction by altering the definition of 'Affiliated Company.' Originally defined as ownership or control exceeding 50%, it's now changed to 25%. This creates a contradiction because other sections of the contract may rely on the original 50% threshold for determining the rights and obligations of affiliated companies. Now due to the modified text in section 1.1, and other provisions that use the term 'Affiliated Company' may now be interpreted differently, creating disputes and uncertainty in their enforcement. For Example, Section 20.1, which specifies that a party can transfer the agreement to an affiliated company, could now be triggered by a much lower ownership percentage, potentially altering the agreement's scope and impact.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 20.1 Successors, Assigns This Agreement and the rights and obligations specified herein shall be binding upon the Parties and their respective legal successors and neither Party shall sell, transfer or assign this Agreement or any part, interest, right or obligation hereunder except that a Party shall have the right to transfer or assign this Agreement in whole (but not in part) to an Affiliated Company provided that: a) such Affiliated Company expressly assumes, by written instrument, all of the obligations of the Party under this Agreement and thereby becomes a Party to this Agreement, and b) such Affiliated Company has adequate financial strength, resources and experience in the reasonable opinion of the other Party (such opinion to be obtained in writing in advance of any assignment), to comply with its obligations under this Agreement. Such assignment shall not release the assigning Party of its obligations under article 11 of this Agreement. No person other than a Party to this Agreement shall acquire any rights hereunder as a third-Party beneficiary or otherwise by virtue of this Agreement. Both Parties will remain independent and competing companies, will develop their own market strategy, and will address the market and the subscribers directly and independently.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Affiliated Company' from requiring over 50% ownership to over 25%. This directly contradicts Section 20.1, which allows assignment of the agreement to an Affiliated Company. The lower threshold now triggers the assignment clause with a significantly reduced ownership stake, altering the original intent and creating a strong in-text contradiction as the rights and obligations change based on the new definition.

perturbation.original_text: 9.2.4 ELEPHANT TALK shall pay all invoices no later than *** after the invoice date. ELEPHANT TALK ***, in the event that ELEPHANT TALK fails to meet ***
perturbation.changed_text: 9.2.4 ELEPHANT TALK shall pay all invoices no later than sixty (60) days after the invoice date. ELEPHANT TALK ***, in the event that ELEPHANT TALK fails to meet ***
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by setting a fixed payment deadline of sixty (60) days in section 9.2.4, while the original text had an unspecified, redacted timeline. The inconsistency arises because other sections might implicitly assume a different or more flexible payment timeframe based on the commercial arrangements in place and ELEPHANT TALK's ***, leading to uncertainty in enforcement. Specifically, section 9.2.8 discusses interest on invoices not paid within the time limit set down in article 9.2.4, but does not specify any days, the modified payment terms will directly contradict any other arrangements and make it unsure whether the legal commercial interest rate plus 1% applied to overdue invoices.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.2.8 Invoices not paid within the time limit set down in article 9.2.4 shall bear interest ("enkelvoudig") on a daily basis at the rate of the legal commercial interest rate ("wettelijke handelsrente") plus 1 % until full payment is received by T-Mobile whether before or after judgment. Interest shall continue to accrue notwithstanding termination or expiry of this Agreement for any cause whatsoever.
perturbation.justification: The original text in section 9.2.4 had a redacted timeline for invoice payments, represented by '***'. The changed text replaces this with a fixed payment deadline of 'sixty (60) days'. This directly contradicts section 9.2.8, which refers to 'the time limit set down in article 9.2.4' for interest accrual on overdue invoices. By fixing the timeline where it was previously variable or undefined, the change introduces a contradiction as it alters the basis on which interest is calculated and potentially conflicts with pre-existing or implied agreements concerning payment deadlines.

perturbation.original_text: 17.2 The Agreement may be terminated by both Parties with a notification period of *** before the end of the Initial Term of the Agreement. If no termination notice is provided, the Agreement will be automatically extended for consecutive *** periods until such time as *** termination notice is provided. At the end of *** Parties will negotiate in good faith regarding a possible extension of the Initial Term. Parties can mutually agree in writing to deviate from an automatic extension of *** by extending this Agreement for a longer period than ***.
perturbation.changed_text: 17.2 The Agreement may be terminated by both Parties with a notification period of one year (365 days) before the end of the Initial Term of the Agreement. If no termination notice is provided, the Agreement will be automatically extended for consecutive one-year periods until such time as a written termination notice is provided. At the end of each year Parties will negotiate in good faith regarding a possible extension of the Initial Term. Parties can mutually agree in writing to deviate from an automatic extension of one year by extending this Agreement for a longer period than one year.
perturbation.explanation: This modification creates an in-text contradiction by replacing the redacted termination notification period with a specific timeframe of one year (365 days) in section 17.2. This alteration conflicts with the contract's general provisions allowing for modifications to the Agreement, as it introduces a rigid termination timeline. Other sections of the contract allow for mutual modifications and amendments, Parties shall enter into good faith negotiations in order to determine if a modification of the Agreement is necessary and if so agree upon the way to implement the required changes in or modifications to this Agreement. (19.2)
perturbation.contradicted_text: 19.2 If a Party requests a modification of this Agreement because (a) a material change occurs in the laws and regulations governing telecommunications in the Netherlands, or (b) a material change (including enforcement action by any regulatory authority) occurs which affects or reasonably could affect the commercial and technical basis of this Agreement, Parties shall enter into good faith negotiations in order to determine if a modification of the Agreement is necessary and if so agree upon the way to implement the required changes in or modifications to this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for a flexible notification period ('***'), while the changed text specifies a fixed one-year notification period. This creates a contradiction because Section 19.2 allows for modifications to the agreement based on changing circumstances. The rigid one-year termination notice in the changed text contradicts the general provisions allowing for modifications to the agreement as stated in Section 19.2: 'Parties shall enter into good faith negotiations in order to determine if a modification of the Agreement is necessary and if so agree upon the way to implement the required changes in or modifications to this Agreement.'
